title_en: Nikah and Family Law in the Shafi'i School
title_ar: النكاح وأحكام الأسرة في المذهب الشافعي
content_en:
The family law section of Rawdat al-Talibin covers the Shafi'i positions on marriage, divorce, and related personal status matters with the comprehensive detail required of a reference work for judges and jurists. Al-Nawawi's treatment is particularly authoritative because he harmonizes the positions of earlier Shafi'i authorities and identifies the correct (mu'tamad) school position on contested questions.

On marriage, the Shafi'i school requires the wali (guardian) as a pillar of the nikah contract — without a valid wali, the marriage is void. Al-Nawawi explains the order of priority among walis: the father, then the paternal grandfather, then other male agnatic relatives in order. The position that a woman cannot contract her own marriage — even if she is an adult and a widow — distinguishes the Shafi'i and Hanbali schools from the Hanafi view that a legally competent adult woman may do so. The Shafi'i position is based on the hadith 'Any woman who marries without the permission of her guardian, her marriage is invalid, invalid, invalid' (Abu Dawud, Tirmidhi).

The mahr (dower) chapter covers the minimum valid mahr — the Shafi'i school does not specify a minimum amount and holds that any item of value, even something small, is a valid mahr — as well as the woman's right to the full mahr after consummation and to half the specified mahr if divorced before consummation. Al-Nawawi addresses the contested question of what happens if no mahr was specified at the time of contract, ruling that mahr al-mithl (the customary mahr for a woman of similar standing) becomes due.

The divorce and iddah chapters cover the Shafi'i positions on revocable and irrevocable divorce, the iddah of a free woman versus a slave woman, the iddah of a pregnant woman, and the rules for establishing paternity during the iddah. Custody (hadanah) follows: the Shafi'i school gives the mother priority for young children until the child reaches the age of discernment (approximately seven), at which point the child may choose which parent to live with.
